A React Data Grid is often the centerpiece of enterprise applications. Whether you’re building a CRM, ERP, financial dashboard, inventory system, or analytics platform, users spend a significant portion of their time interacting with tabular data.
While features like sorting, filtering, virtualization, and row grouping are essential, styling is what transforms a functional grid into a polished, user-friendly experience.
A few years ago, styling a data grid mainly involved changing colors and fonts. In 2026, expectations have evolved. Modern React applications require responsive themes, accessibility, dark mode support, conditional formatting, design system integration, and performance-conscious styling.
In this article, we’ll explore modern styling techniques for React Data Grids, compare today’s leading libraries, and explain why choosing a framework with a mature theming system can significantly reduce development time.
Why Styling Matters More Than Ever
Enterprise users spend hours every day inside data-intensive applications.
A well-designed React Data Grid improves:
Readability
Productivity
Accessibility
Error detection
Visual consistency
User satisfaction
Good styling isn’t just about aesthetics — it helps users understand and interact with complex datasets more efficiently.
Core Styling Techniques for React Data Grids
Most modern React Data Grid libraries support multiple approaches to customization.

• Cell Styling
Cell-level styling is ideal when formatting depends on the value inside a cell.
Examples include:
Highlighting overdue invoices
Color-coding financial gains and losses
Displaying completed tasks in green
Emphasizing critical alerts
Instead of applying styles globally, developers can dynamically style individual cells based on business rules.
Common use cases:
Currency formatting
Status indicators
KPI dashboards
Risk scoring
Performance metrics

• Row Styling
Sometimes the entire record deserves emphasis rather than a single value.
Examples:
High-priority orders
Suspended customers
Failed transactions
Selected records
Recently updated items
Row-level styling improves scanability and helps users identify important information quickly.

• CSS Classes
Modern applications rarely rely solely on inline styles.
Reusable CSS classes offer:
Better maintainability
Cleaner code
Easier theming
Design consistency
This approach works especially well when integrating with design systems or component libraries.

• Conditional Formatting
Conditional styling has become standard in enterprise dashboards.
Examples include:
Green for healthy metrics
Yellow for warnings
Red for critical issues
Conditional formatting allows users to identify trends and anomalies without reading every value individually.

• Theme Customization
Modern React applications usually support multiple visual themes.
Popular options include:
Light mode
Dark mode
High-contrast accessibility mode
Brand-specific themes
Instead of rewriting CSS, many grid libraries now expose design tokens or theme variables for quick customization.
Modern Styling Trends in 2026
React Data Grid styling continues to evolve alongside modern UI development.
Some of the biggest trends include:
Design Tokens
Organizations increasingly use centralized design tokens for:
Colors
Typography
Spacing
Border radius
Shadows
This keeps grids visually consistent with the rest of the application.
Dark Mode
Dark mode has shifted from a nice-to-have feature to an expected standard.
A well-designed grid should automatically adapt:
Backgrounds
Borders
Hover states
Selection colors
Text contrast
Accessibility First
Accessibility is now a baseline requirement for enterprise software.
Modern grids should support:
WCAG 2.2 compliance
Keyboard navigation
Focus indicators
Screen readers
High-contrast themes
Color-independent visual cues
Responsive Layouts
Users increasingly access business applications on tablets and hybrid devices.
Responsive grids now support:
Adaptive column widths
Flexible layouts
Mobile-friendly interactions
Touch optimization
Styling Without Sacrificing Performance
Large datasets present unique challenges.
Heavy CSS or unnecessary DOM updates can slow rendering dramatically.
Best practices include:
Use virtualization
Prefer CSS classes over excessive inline styles
Avoid expensive DOM manipulations
Minimize unnecessary re-renders
Use conditional styling efficiently
Modern React Data Grid libraries are designed to handle these optimizations automatically.
Popular React Data Grid Solutions in 2026
Several mature libraries provide extensive styling capabilities.
AG Grid
AG Grid offers flexible styling through:
Cell styles
Row styles
CSS classes
Class rules
Theme customization
Dark mode support
Its styling system remains one of the most comprehensive in the React ecosystem.
MUI X Data Grid
MUI X integrates seamlessly with Material UI.
Benefits include:
Theme Provider support
Design token integration
Custom renderers
Responsive layouts
Material Design consistency
TanStack Table
TanStack Table focuses on logic rather than presentation.
Developers gain complete freedom over styling, making it an excellent choice for highly customized interfaces.
Material React Table
Material React Table provides polished defaults while allowing developers to customize nearly every visual aspect using Material UI components.
Ext JS React Data Grid
For organizations building enterprise-scale software, Ext JS takes a different approach.
Rather than providing only a data grid, it offers a complete enterprise UI framework with:
Advanced theming
Built-in dark mode
Rich CSS customization
Spreadsheet features
Pivot grids
Charts
Forms
Dashboards
Tree grids
Scheduler components
Because all UI components share the same design language, teams can maintain visual consistency across large applications without assembling multiple third-party libraries.
Styling Best Practices
Regardless of the library you choose, these principles remain valuable:
Keep styling consistent across the application.
Use color to communicate meaning — not decoration.
Prefer reusable CSS classes over duplicated styles.
Maintain sufficient contrast for accessibility.
Test themes in both light and dark modes.
Avoid excessive animations inside large datasets.
Design for scalability as datasets grow.
These practices improve both usability and maintainability.
Choosing the Right React Data Grid
Every project has different requirements.
If you’re building:
Lightweight dashboards → TanStack Table or Material React Table may be sufficient.
Material Design applications → MUI X is a natural fit.
Highly customizable enterprise grids → AG Grid provides extensive flexibility.
Large business applications requiring data grids, forms, charts, scheduling, pivot tables, and unified theming → Ext JS offers a comprehensive UI framework that can reduce integration complexity while delivering enterprise-grade capabilities.
The best choice depends on your application’s scale, team expertise, and long-term maintenance goals.
Final Thoughts
Styling a React Data Grid in 2026 goes far beyond changing fonts or background colors. Modern enterprise applications require responsive layouts, accessible themes, conditional formatting, design system integration, and high-performance rendering — all while maintaining a consistent user experience.
Leading solutions like AG Grid, MUI X, TanStack Table, and Material React Table each offer strong styling capabilities tailored to different development needs. For organizations building large-scale enterprise software with extensive UI requirements, Ext JS stands out by combining advanced React Data Grid functionality with a complete UI framework, helping teams deliver cohesive, maintainable applications without relying on a fragmented collection of libraries.
Ultimately, the right styling strategy is one that balances usability, performance, accessibility, and maintainability — creating interfaces that help users work faster and make better decisions.
